coredata.dc:title: Detection of tetracycline antibiotics using fluorescent “Turn-off” sensor based on S, N-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Yao
Qiao, Wenjun
Long, Wanjun
Chen, Hengye
Fu, Haiyan
Zhou, Chunsong
She, Yuanbin
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, antibiotic residues in food have been of great concern to regulators and consumers. In this study, a novel fluorescent sensor based on S, N-doped carbon quantum dots (S, N-CQDs) was established for rapid detection of tetracycline antibiotics (TCs). Through the internal filter effect (IFE), QDs fluorescence can be effectively quenched by TCs, endowing it an “off” condition. Under the optimal conditions, the TC concentration in the range of 1.88–60 μmol/L had a good linear relationship with the change of QDs fluorescence intensity, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.56 μmol/L (S/N = 3). Furthermore, the proposed “Turn-off” sensor could be employed to quickly and accurately quantify TCs residues even in milk, honey and tap water. The recovery rate was as high as between 93.61% and 102.31%. The established sensor has great application value in the fields of food safety and drug analysis, and provides broad prospects for the future food industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S, N-doped carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence sensor
Turn off
Tetracycline antibiotics

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM and particle size image of the S, N-CQDs; (B) FT-IR spectra of S, N-CQDs; (C) XPS spectra of S, N-CQDs; XPS spectrum of C1s (D), N1s (E), O1s (F), S2p (G).

(A) Fluorescence excitation, emission spectrum and UV–vis absorption spectrum of S, N-CQDs; (B) Photoluminescence spectrum of S, N-CQDs; (C) The fluorescence quenching rate ((F0- F1) / F0) of fluorescence probe via time with the addition of 80 μmol/L TC; (D) Effect of pH on the detection of TC by S, N-CQDs.

(A) The effect of various antibiotics and small biological molecules (1.0 × 10-4 mol/L) on the S, N-CQDs; (B) The interference of different metal ions (1.0 × 10-3 mol/L) on the experimental system; (C) When the TC concentrations (0–600 μmol/L) increases gradually, Fluorescence emission spectrum of S, N-CQDs; (D) the curve of the (F0-F1)/F0 vs. TC concentration. Inset: Calibration curve of relative fluorescence intensity and TC.
(A) The fluorescence decay curve of S, N-CQDs with and without TC; (B) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of TC and fluorescence excitation, emission spectra of S, N-CQDs.
Compare various methods of TCs detection.
Detection of TC in milk, honey and tap water.

head.ce:title: Detection of tetracycline antibiotics using fluorescent “Turn-off” sensor based on S, N-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology, College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology, College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Environmental Protection City Technology Limited Company (IEPCT), Yixing 214200, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Environmental Protection City Technology Limited Company (IEPCT)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Environmental Protection City Technology Limited Company (IEPCT), Yixing 214200, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, antibiotic residues in food have been of great concern to regulators and consumers. In this study, a novel fluorescent sensor based on S, N-doped carbon quantum dots (S, N-CQDs) was established for rapid detection of tetracycline antibiotics (TCs). Through the internal filter effect (IFE), QDs fluorescence can be effectively quenched by TCs, endowing it an “off” condition. Under the optimal conditions, the TC concentration in the range of 1.88–60 μmol/L had a good linear relationship with the change of QDs fluorescence intensity, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.56 μmol/L (S/N = 3). Furthermore, the proposed “Turn-off” sensor could be employed to quickly and accurately quantify TCs residues even in milk, honey and tap water. The recovery rate was as high as between 93.61% and 102.31%. The established sensor has great application value in the fields of food safety and drug analysis, and provides broad prospects for the future food industry.

ce:section.ce:para: Veterinary antibiotics are a generic name for a class of drugs used to treat or prevent livestock diseases or to increase economic efficiency by promoting animal growth. Antibiotics are widely used in animal husbandry, aquaculture, pharmaceutical industry, etc. [1-3]. In recent years, the problem of antibiotic residues in edible animal products has been of great concern to regulators and consumers [4]. Under the premise of standardizing the use of antibiotics, antibiotics will be metabolized or excreted along with the animal’s metabolism, thereby reducing their residual levels in the animal’s body. However, if driven by economic benefits, the antibiotics have not been used following the regulations, which will cause the antibiotic content in animals to exceed the standard [5]. Tetracycline antibiotics (TCs), as an important class of antibiotics, have been widely used due to their good antibacterial properties, low cost, low side effects, and good therapeutic effects [6]. However, TCs are difficult to be degraded and may accumulate in the soil, water, and foods such as meat, eggs, and milk, thus endangering human health and the ecological environment through the food chain and ecological cycle [7-9]. Therefore, it is particularly important to establish a method for rapid and accurate detection of TCs.

At present, many methods for detecting TCs have been developed, including chromatography, immunoassay, and spectroscopy. Among them, chromatography includes high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10,11] and liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [12,13]. Chromatography has high separation efficiency and low detection limit, but usually requires expensive instruments, time consuming and consumable, and depends on professional testers. Immunoassay includes enzyme-linked immunoassay (ELISA) [14], fluorescence polarization immunoassay [15], and Colloidal gold immunochromatography [16]. The immunoassay has the advantages of strong specificity, high sensitivity, and large-scale detection. However, it has poor selectivity and high requirements for antibody synthesis, which limits the cost of simultaneous detection. Spectroscopy includes ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry (UV) [17], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [18], and fluorescence spectroscopy (FS) [19,20]. Spectroscopy is simple to operation, strong in timeliness, and has a low detection limit, but it is susceptible to peak overlap, and its specificity is poor. Fluorescence has attracted more and more attention in recent years. Although Tan [19] and Anand [20] have developed fluorescent methods for the rapid and selective detection of TCs, their probe synthesis still has the disadvantages of high energy consumption and high cost. Therefore, there is an urgent need for a tetracycline detection method with high selectivity, fast sensitivity, high accuracy and low cost.

The size of quantum dots is close to the size of dye molecules commonly used in the past, so it is of great use for biomedical research, like fluorescent dyes. Among them, CQDs are small in size (below 10 nm) and consist of dispersed spherical carbon particles, which are novel nano-carbon materials with fluorescent properties [21-23]. In recent years, CQDs have attracted wide attention from researchers due to their many advantages such as excellent optical properties, high stability, long fluorescence lifetime and good biocompatibility [24-26]. In addition, CQDs as fluorescent sensors have strong specificity ability [27]. Many research groups have used CQDs as fluorescent probes and applied them to the measurement of metal ions [28,29], small molecules [30,31], drug delivery [32,33] and electrochemical luminescence [34,35], etc. In addition, it has been reported that CQDs synthesized by S and N doping have higher quantum yields [28,35,36]. Xue et. al has established a carbon dot-based fluorescent probe for sensing tetracycline, however, the probe was too sensitive to pH, which may limit its practical application [37]. Uriarte et. al also proposed an effect probe using carbon dots based on glycerol and urea, but they only explored its application in the determination of tetracycline in a simple matrix (urine samples) [38].

Herein, we used citric acid monohydrate and glutathione to synthesize a low-cost, simple and environmentally friendly S, N-CQDs by hydrothermal method, and developed it as a fluorescence sensor to detect TC with high selectivity and sensitivity. Under the optimized conditions, S, N-CQDs could specifically bind TCs including TC, OTC, and CTC and its fluorescence was quenched through internal filter effect (IFE), thereby achieving the identification and quantification of TCs. More importantly, the proposed sensor could quickly and accurately detect tetracycline residues even in complex food matrices such as milk and honey, which provides a new perspective of, as well as guidance for the antibiotic residue monitoring in the food industry.

Tetracycline (TC), Oxytetracycline (OTC), Chlortetracycline (CTC), Chloramphenicol (CHL), Neomycin (NEO), Streptomycin (STR), Kanamycin (KAN), Gentamicin (GEN), Enrofloxacin (ENR), and Pefloxacin (NOR) were supplied by Shanghai Maclean's Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Gallic acid (GA), reduced glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), histidine (His), glutamic acid (Glu), arginine (Arg), aspartic acid (Asp), lysine (Lys), serine (Ser), threonine (Thr), and spectral-grade potassium bromide (KBr) powder were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid monohydrate (CA), glucose (Glc), ascorbic acid (AA), metal ions (Ca2+、Mg2+、Mn2+、Al3+、Na+、K+、Zn2+、Cu2+、Co2+、Pb2+、Fe3+、Fe2+、Ni2+、Cr3+、Cd2+、Hg2+、Ag+) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Milk and honey were supplied by a local supermarket (Wuhan, China). All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water. The pH values were calibrated with a hydrochloric acid pH meter.

The morphology and particle size of S, N-CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, TECNAI G2 20 S-TWIN, Oregon, USA). The fluorescence spectrum was obtained by scanning with an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High-Tech Company, Tokyo, Japan). UV–Vis spectra were measured by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800) (MaPADA, Shanghai, China). FT-IR spectra were obtained from a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (6700-FT-IR) (Thermo Fisher Nicolet, Massachusetts, USA). The Fluorescence lifetime was measured using a steady-state/transient FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Edinburgh, Britain). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy is obtained by X-Photoelectron Spectrometer (VG Multilab 2000, Thermo electron corporation, Massachusetts, USA). An electrothermal constant temperature blast drying oven (Senxin DGG-9030B, Shanghai, China) was used for the synthesis experiment. Centrifugation test was performed using High speed refrigerated microcentrifuge (SCILOGEX D3024R, Connecticut, USA).

The preparation of S, N-CQDs was minor modified by the reported method [25]. Briefly, 0.2101 g CA and 0.7683 g GSH were added to 30 mL of ultrapure water, mixed well and placed in the reaction kettle, hydrothermally reacted for 6 h in an electric heating constant temperature air drying oven at 200 °C, and cooled to room temperature. The S, N-CQDs solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Then transferred to a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 3500 Da and dialyzed in the dark for 24 h. The water was changed every 6 h. Slightly yellow clear S, N-CQDs were obtained, diluted 1.5 × 104 times with ultrapure water, and stored at 4 °C.

Under the excitation of S, N-CQDs at 340 nm, the fluorescence emission peak wavelength was 412 nm. The fluorescence measurement method of TCs is as follows. S, N-CQDs stock solution (100 μL) was added to 800 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.05 mol/L, pH = 4.0), TCs solution (100 μL) of different concentrations was added, and mixed at room temperature for 3 min. The fluorescence intensity after mixing was measured. Under the excitation wavelength of 340 nm, the voltage of 700 V and the slit width of 10 nm, the fluorescence spectrum data were collected in the range of 360–600 nm.

The selectivity of other antibiotics and small biological molecules for TCs was tested. S, N-CQDs stock solution (100 μL) was added to 800 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.05 mol/L, pH = 4.0), 100 μL TC, OTC, CTC, CHL, NEO, STR, KAN, GEN, ENR, NOR, GA, GSH, Cys, His, Glu, Arg, Asp, Lys, Ser and Thr (1.0 × 10-4 mol/L) solutions were added respectively. The fluorescence interference of metal ions on S, N-CQDs detection TCs was studied. The following metal ions were tested: Ca2+、Mg2+、Mn2+、Al3+、Na+、K+、Zn2+、Cu2+、Co2+、Pb2+、Fe3+、Fe2+、Ni2+、Cr3+、Cd2+、Hg2+、Ag+ (1.0 × 10-3 mol/L).

Milk, honey and tap water samples were selected as authenticity samples for this study. To prepare a milk sample [39], different amounts of TC were spiked into skimmed pure milk (5 mL) and mixed with 20 mL of McIlvaine buffer containing 20 mmol/L EDTA (pH = 5.0) for pretreatment. Firstly, added 2 mL of trichloroacetic acid to the milk sample, the milk sample was degreased, and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min for deproteinization. Then, 1 mol/L NaOH was used to adjust the pH to 7.2. Finally, it was filtered through 0.22 μm microporous membrane before being used for the analysis of TC. In order to prepare honey samples, metal ions such as calcium, iron, magnesium, and zinc present in the honey need to be removed [40]. Firstly, pretreatment with 5 g of honey and 25 mL of PBS-EDTA at pH = 7.2. Different amounts of TC were then spiked into the prepared honey solution and used for this research. The tap water was filtered using a microporous membrane. Different concentrations of tetracycline were added for detection.

The morphology and size of S, N-CQDs were characterized by TEM. The prepared S, N-CQDs (Fig. 1 A) have good dispersibility and their morphology is spherical. In addition, the average size of 100 individual S, N-CQDs (Nano Measurer 1.2 software) was analyzed. The diameter of S, N-CQDs is around 2.5 nm, and the average range of 1.0 to 4.5 nm.

Fig. 1B was the result of Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of S, N-CQDs. The characteristic peak at 3432 cm−1 is due to the tensile vibrations of –OH and –NH2 [41]. At 2928 cm−1, it may be due to the stretching vibration of the C-H bond caused by methyl and methylene groups [38]. In addition, the absorption peak at 1377 cm−1 indicates the bending vibration of C-H [42]. At 1643 cm−1, it was attributed to stretching of C = O. At 1549 cm−1, it was tensile vibration of C-N. 2365 cm−1 and 1230 cm−1 are absorption bands of S-H and C-S-C tensile vibrations [43]. In summary, it can prove that S, N-CQDs contain functional groups such as carboxyl and amino groups.

To determine the elemental composition and surface chemical properties of S, N-CQDs, they were further characterized by XPS. The results of the XPS full scan spectrum in Fig. 1C have shown that there are four different characteristic peaks near 163.8, 284.8, 399.9 and 531.4 eV, which belong to the S2p, C1s, N1s, and O1s signals, respectively. The contents were 0.53%, 67.62%, 15.48% and 16.37%, respectively. The C1s scan spectrum (Fig. 1D) has four peaks, which correspond to C-C/C = C (284.21 eV), C-S (284.93 eV), C-N (285.88 eV), and C = O (287.85 eV) functional group [44]. The N1s scan spectrum (Fig. 1E) has two characteristic peaks at 399.11 and 400.10 eV, which correspond to N-H and C-N-C, respectively [45]. The O1s scan spectrum (Fig. 1F) contains three peaks corresponding to C = O (531.12 eV), C-O-H (531.83 eV) and O-C = O (533.37 eV), respectively [46,47]. The scanning spectrum of the lower binding energy S2p (Fig. 1G) can be divided into two main peaks, corresponding to S2p1/2 (163.71 eV) and S2p3/2 (164.67 eV) [43,44]. The results have shown that the prepared S, N-CQDs have abundant amino and carboxyl functional groups. In short, the results of XPS are consistent with FT-IR.

The fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra in Fig. 2 A were used to verify the optical properties of S, N-CQDs. The characteristic absorption peaks of S, N-CQDs are located at 204 nm and 323 nm. The spectrum peak at 204 nm is attributed to the n-σ * transition of –NH2 and -SH [45]. The absorption peak at 323 nm can be attributed to the n-π* transition of C = O [48]. The maximum absorption wavelength in the UV–Vis spectrum is 204 nm. Fig. 2B has shown that the surface states and size distributions of S, N-CQDs are different, and they have fluorescence behavior related to excitation [49]. The excitation wavelength is between 310 nm and 370 nm, and the emission wavelength of S, N-CQDs gradually becomes longer. The optimal excitation of S, N-CQDs is 340 nm. and the strongest emission peak appears at 412 nm.

S, N-CQDs stability test over time (Fig. S1) was performed under ultraviolet (365 nm) irradiation. After 80 min of continuous irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of S, N-CQDs hardly changed, indicating that S, N-CQDs had high stability. Fig. S2 proved that the prepared S, N-CQDs have good salt stability. There was no significant change in fluorescence intensity when exposed to high concentrations of sodium chloride (0–1.0 × 10-3 mol/L).

For high sensitivity detection of TCs, the experimental conditions (pH and reaction time) were optimized. After adding TC to the S, N-CQDs fluorescent probe, the fluorescence quenching rate ((F0-F1)/F0) after different reaction times was observed. F0 is the fluorescence intensity of TC is not added to S, N-CQDs, F1 is the fluorescence intensity of TC is added to S, N-CQDs. Fig. 2C has shown that the fluorescence intensity of S, N-CQDs quenched by TC changed greatly within 1 min and gradually stabilized within 1 to 10 min. In order to detect TC quickly and stably, we have selected 3 min as the best response time. In addition, the pH of the solution is also important for detection. Tris-HCl buffer solution with different pH values (pH = 3.0–9.0) were selected for comparison. When pH = 4.0, TC quenches S N-CQDs has reached its maximum potential (Fig. 2D), and the acidity or alkalinity has no beneficial reaction. Therefore, pH = 4.0 was selected as the substrate solution for detection.

The selectivity of S, N-CQDs fluorescent sensor for TCs detection was studied. We have selected different types of antibiotics (TC, OTC, CTC, CHL, NEO, STR, KAN, GEN, ENR, NOR) and biological small molecules (GA, GSH, Cys, His, Glu, Arg, Asp, Lys, Ser, Thr) to interact with the fluorescent sensor at a concentration of 1.0 × 10-4 mol/L. Under the same experimental conditions, the selected other substances fail to produce obvious interfering phenomenons towards the S, N-CQDs fluorescent sensor (Fig. 3 A), which confirmed that the new S, N-CQDs fluorescence sensor can be used to determine tetracycline antibiotics.

In addition, Fig. 3B has shown that the interference of other metal ions on the experimental system. We added different kinds of metal ions (Ca2+、Mg2+、Mn2+、Al3+、Na+、K+、Zn2+、Cu2+、Co2+、Pb2+、Fe3+、Fe2+、Ni2+、Cr3+、Cd2+、Hg2+、Ag+) , where the concentration of the solution is 1.0 × 10-3 mol/L. There was no significant effect on the reaction system in the presence of high concentrations of interfering substances. Therefore, it has further demonstrated the satisfactory selectivity of this scheme.

Under the optimal conditions, we tested the response to different concentrations of TCs and proved the sensitivity of the developed S, N-CQDs in TCs detection. As shown in Fig. 3C, the TC concentration increased from 0 to 600 μmol/L, and the fluorescence intensity of S, N-CQDs gradually decreased. At the same time, Fig. 3D has shown that the curve of the (F0-F1)/F0 vs. TC concentration of 0–1000 μmol/L. Moreover, there was a good linear relationship between the TC concentration range of 1.88–60 μmol/L and the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0-F1)/F0. The calibration curve was ((F0-F1)/F0 = 0.01796 + 0.00687CTC, R2 = 0.998), and the LOD was calculated as 0.56 μmol/L (S/N = 3). Therefore, S, N-CQDs fluorescence sensor has good sensitivity to TC. Table 1 compared various analytical methods for TC detection. It is comparable to or better than other fluorescent quantum dot sensing methods [51,53]. In addition, our method is not affected by other antibiotics and small biological molecules and the interference of metal ions.

The reaction mechanism of S, N-CQDs fluorescence sensor to detect TC was analyzed. The Stern-Volmer equation was as follow:
F0/F1 = 1 + kqτ0 [Q]

where kq is the rate coefficient of TC, τ0 is the fluorescence emission excited state lifetime of S, N-CQDs without TC, and [Q] is the concentration of TC. Therefore, it is calculated that kq is 1.14 × 1012 L·mol−1·s−1, and the kq value is greater than 2.0 × 1010 L·mol−1·s−1, which has indicated the possibility of static quenching. As we can see, the fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 4 A) of the prepared S, N-CQDs was 9.55 ns, and the change was not obvious after adding 100 μmol/L TC, thus further proving the possibility of static quenching [50]. At the same time, we used a UV–visible spectrophotometer to detect the UV absorption spectrum of TC. Fig. 4B has shown that the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of TC coincides with the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of S, N-CQDs, which eventually caused the absorber TC to absorb the emission light of S, N-CQD. It can be proved that there is the possibility of internal filtration effect (IFE). In addition, we compared the FT-IR spectra of S, N-CQDs without and with TC added (Fig. 1B), and found that the absorption peaks of S, N-CQDs did not change significantly. After adding TC, the characteristic absorption peaks of S, N-CQD at 3432 cm−1, 1643 cm−1, 2365 cm−1, and 1230 cm−1 showed no obvious blue shift or red shift, and no new absorption peaks. It was proved that no new functional groups were produced, which further confirmed the occurrence of internal filtration effect [51]. However, the IFE is not within the scope of static or dynamic quenching [55]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the sensor detects TCs by IFE mechanism.

We further verified the practical application of S, N-CQDs sensor for TCs detection. Spiked samples were prepared by adding different concentrations of TC to the actual samples (milk, honey, tap water) and the recovery rate of TC was adopted to evaluate the accuracy of the qualitative analysis. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery rate of TC in milk, honey, and tap water was 93.61%-102.31%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) did not exceed 3.52% (n = 3), and satisfactory results were obtained. Therefore, it can be shown that the fluorescence sensor system has great potential for detecting tetracycline antibiotics in complex matrices.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized photoluminescence S, N-CQDs using citric acid monohydrate and reduced glutathione. And proved that it can be used for the detection of TCs through the IFE mechanism. Compared with other reported conventional methods, our synthesized S, N-CQDs are simple to prepare and low in cost. In addition, this solution does not require complicated design, and has the advantages of fast and timely, high sensitivity, and strong selectivity. At the same time, the S, N-CQDs fluorescence sensor has shown satisfactory results in the detection of trace TCs in complex environments and real samples. All these results showed that the S, N-CQDs fluorescence sensor can detect tetracycline antibiotics satisfactorily. The proposed method has potential applications in the field of food and drug analysis.

Yao Fan: Investigation, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Wenjun Qiao: Investigation. Wanjun Long: . Hengye Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Haiyan Fu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Chunsong Zhou: Resources. Yuanbin She: Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants No. 32122068, 31972164). The National Research Initiation Fund of Zhejiang University of Technology (No. 2020101005929).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121033.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Biosens. Bioelectron.

Nanoscale

Anal. Chim. Acta
